input.content: You are an expert MLOps data-quality engineer specializing in computer vision datasets. When asked to analyze class imbalance, use the search_recent_research tool to find supporting evidence for your recommendations before giving your final answer.

You are an expert MLOps data-quality engineer.

A user has a COCO-format computer-vision dataset with the following class distribution:
  - Anemone (id=1): 11 annotations
  - Arctic Scallop (alive) (id=2): 880 annotations
  - Arctic Scallop (dead) (id=3): 865 annotations
  - Blue Mussel (id=4): 9 annotations
  - Brittle Star (id=5): 1649 annotations
  - Clam (alive) (id=6): 433 annotations
  - Clam (dead) (id=7): 1240 annotations
  - Cockle Clam (alive) (id=8): 59 annotations
  - Cockle Clam (dead) (id=9): 38 annotations
  - Green Urchin (alive) (id=10): 136 annotations
  - Green Urchin (dead) (id=11): 7 annotations
  - Gunnel (id=12): 19 annotations
  - Marine Sponges (id=13): 33 annotations
  - Sculpin (id=14): 2 annotations
  - Sea Cucumber (id=15): 39 annotations
  - Seastar (id=16): 13 annotations
  - Toad Crab (id=18): 2 annotations
  - Tunicate (id=19): 6 annotations
  - Tunicates (id=20): 30 annotations
  - Unknown (id=21): 10 annotations

The dataset will be used for: counting marine organisms from drop camera survey images around the Belcher islands to support fisheries and conservation efforts

Analyze the dataset for class imbalance and bias. Consider REAL-WORLD domain context — for
example, if the classes represent species, consider natural species abundance; if they
represent medical conditions, consider disease prevalence; and so on.

Guidelines:
- Prefer UPSAMPLING minority classes over downsampling majority classes, unless the dataset
  is very large (>10 000 images per class).
- Aim for a distribution that makes sense for the stated fine-tuning purpose, NOT
  necessarily a perfectly uniform distribution.
- For each class, specify a concrete target annotation count, the strategy
  ("upsample", "downsample", or "keep"), and a short rationale.
- Provide a brief (2-4 sentence) overall strategy blurb.
- Use the search_recent_research tool to find relevant papers supporting your recommendations.

Respond with ONLY a JSON object (no markdown fences) in this exact schema:
{
  "analysis": "<overall strategy blurb>",
  "classes": [
    {
      "class_name": "<name>",
      "current_count": <int>,
      "target_count": <int>,
      "strategy": "upsample | downsample | keep",
      "rationale": "<why>"
    }
  ]
}

queries: class imbalance strategies for marine species detection and counting in computer vision
handling long-tail distribution in benthic image datasets for fisheries conservation
upsampling vs downsampling for rare species in object detection datasets
impact of class imbalance on counting accuracy in ecological monitoring computer vision

output.content:
{
  "analysis": "The strategy addresses the extreme long-tail distribution typical of benthic surveys by establishing a minimum representation floor for rare taxa to ensure detection viability, while maintaining the relative proportions of dominant species to reflect ecological reality. We prioritize upsampling via augmentation and oversampling for classes with fewer than 100 instances to prevent the model from collapsing these features into the background or majority classes, which is critical for biodiversity monitoring.",
  "classes": [
    {
      "class_name": "Anemone",
      "current_count": 11,
      "target_count": 100,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Current count is insufficient for the model to learn distinct morphological features; upsampling to 100 provides a baseline for detection."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Arctic Scallop (alive)",
      "current_count": 880,
      "target_count": 880,
      "strategy": "keep",
      "rationale": "Sufficient representation for a primary fishery target; preserves natural abundance variance without requiring further augmentation."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Arctic Scallop (dead)",
      "current_count": 865,
      "target_count": 865,
      "strategy": "keep",
      "rationale": "Balanced with the 'alive' class, allowing the model to effectively learn the visual cues of mortality for population health assessments."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Blue Mussel",
      "current_count": 9,
      "target_count": 100,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Critically under-represented; requires upsampling to ensure the model can distinguish it from other bivalves in complex benthic backgrounds."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Brittle Star",
      "current_count": 1649,
      "target_count": 1649,
      "strategy": "keep",
      "rationale": "Naturally dominant species; high count is beneficial for learning to detect individuals in high-density clusters."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Clam (alive)",
      "current_count": 433,
      "target_count": 433,
      "strategy": "keep",
      "rationale": "Adequate sample size for robust feature extraction in a survey context."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Clam (dead)",
      "current_count": 1240,
      "target_count": 1240,
      "strategy": "keep",
      "rationale": "High count reflects natural accumulation of shells; provides excellent negative-sample training against live clams."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Cockle Clam (alive)",
      "current_count": 59,
      "target_count": 150,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Needs a boost to reach a statistical significance level that prevents confusion with more common clam species."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Cockle Clam (dead)",
      "current_count": 38,
      "target_count": 150,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Upsampling ensures the model can differentiate dead cockles from other dead bivalves, supporting accurate mortality ratios."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Green Urchin (alive)",
      "current_count": 136,
      "target_count": 200,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Slight upsampling to strengthen the feature representation of this ecologically significant herbivore."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Green Urchin (dead)",
      "current_count": 7,
      "target_count": 100,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Extreme imbalance compared to 'alive' urchins; upsampling is necessary to monitor urchin barrens and mortality events."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Gunnel",
      "current_count": 19,
      "target_count": 100,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Small fish are often missed in benthic surveys; upsampling improves recall for mobile fauna."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Marine Sponges",
      "current_count": 33,
      "target_count": 150,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Sponges are key habitat-forming species; increasing count supports better conservation mapping."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Sculpin",
      "current_count": 2,
      "target_count": 100,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Two samples are statistically negligible; upsampling is required to prevent the model from ignoring this predator entirely."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Sea Cucumber",
      "current_count": 39,
      "target_count": 150,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Boosts representation to ensure the model captures the varied orientations and shapes of these organisms."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Seastar",
      "current_count": 13,
      "target_count": 100,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Important predator for scallop populations; requires more samples to ensure accurate counting for fisheries management."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Toad Crab",
      "current_count": 2,
      "target_count": 100,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Critical for biodiversity monitoring; requires significant oversampling or synthetic data to reach a detectable threshold."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Tunicate",
      "current_count": 6,
      "target_count": 100,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Low count risks the class being treated as background; upsampling ensures visibility."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Tunicates",
      "current_count": 30,
      "target_count": 150,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Likely represents colonial forms; upsampling helps the model distinguish between solitary and colonial tunicate structures."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Unknown",
      "current_count": 10,
      "target_count": 10,
      "strategy": "keep",
      "rationale": "Keeping this low prevents the model from using 'Unknown' as a default bucket for difficult-to-classify rare species."
    }
  ]
}

      "class_name": "Green Urchin (dead)",
      "current_count": 7,
      "target_count": 100,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Extreme imbalance compared to 'alive' urchins; upsampling is necessary to monitor urchin barrens and mortality events."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Gunnel",
      "current_count": 19,
      "target_count": 100,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Small fish are often missed in benthic surveys; upsampling improves recall for mobile fauna."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Marine Sponges",
      "current_count": 33,
      "target_count": 150,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Sponges are key habitat-forming species; increasing count supports better conservation mapping."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Sculpin",
      "current_count": 2,
      "target_count": 100,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Two samples are statistically negligible; upsampling is required to prevent the model from ignoring this predator entirely."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Sea Cucumber",
      "current_count": 39,
      "target_count": 150,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Boosts representation to ensure the model captures the varied orientations and shapes of these organisms."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Seastar",
      "current_count": 13,
      "target_count": 100,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Important predator for scallop populations; requires more samples to ensure accurate counting for fisheries management."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Toad Crab",
      "current_count": 2,
      "target_count": 100,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Critical for biodiversity monitoring; requires significant oversampling or synthetic data to reach a detectable threshold."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Tunicate",
      "current_count": 6,
      "target_count": 100,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Low count risks the class being treated as background; upsampling ensures visibility."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Tunicates",
      "current_count": 30,
      "target_count": 150,
      "strategy": "upsample",
      "rationale": "Likely represents colonial forms; upsampling helps the model distinguish between solitary and colonial tunicate structures."
    },
    {
      "class_name": "Unknown",
      "current_count": 10,
      "target_count": 10,
      "strategy": "keep",
      "rationale": "Keeping this low prevents the model from using 'Unknown' as a default bucket for difficult-to-classify rare species."
    }
  ]
}

input_args:
You are an expert MLOps data-quality engineer.

A user has a COCO-format computer-vision dataset with the following class distribution:
  - Anemone (id=1): 11 annotations
  - Arctic Scallop (alive) (id=2): 880 annotations
  - Arctic Scallop (dead) (id=3): 865 annotations
  - Blue Mussel (id=4): 9 annotations
  - Brittle Star (id=5): 1649 annotations
  - Clam (alive) (id=6): 433 annotations
  - Clam (dead) (id=7): 1240 annotations
  - Cockle Clam (alive) (id=8): 59 annotations
  - Cockle Clam (dead) (id=9): 38 annotations
  - Green Urchin (alive) (id=10): 136 annotations
  - Green Urchin (dead) (id=11): 7 annotations
  - Gunnel (id=12): 19 annotations
  - Marine Sponges (id=13): 33 annotations
  - Sculpin (id=14): 2 annotations
  - Sea Cucumber (id=15): 39 annotations
  - Seastar (id=16): 13 annotations
  - Toad Crab (id=18): 2 annotations
  - Tunicate (id=19): 6 annotations
  - Tunicates (id=20): 30 annotations
  - Unknown (id=21): 10 annotations

The dataset will be used for: counting marine organisms from drop camera survey images around the Belcher islands to support fisheries and conservation efforts

Analyze the dataset for class imbalance and bias. Consider REAL-WORLD domain context — for
example, if the classes represent species, consider natural species abundance; if they
represent medical conditions, consider disease prevalence; and so on.

Guidelines:
- Prefer UPSAMPLING minority classes over downsampling majority classes, unless the dataset
  is very large (>10 000 images per class).
- Aim for a distribution that makes sense for the stated fine-tuning purpose, NOT
  necessarily a perfectly uniform distribution.
- For each class, specify a concrete target annotation count, the strategy
  ("upsample", "downsample", or "keep"), and a short rationale.
- Provide a brief (2-4 sentence) overall strategy blurb.
- Use the search_recent_research tool to find relevant papers supporting your recommendations.

Respond with ONLY a JSON object (no markdown fences) in this exact schema:
{
  "analysis": "<overall strategy blurb>",
  "classes": [
    {
      "class_name": "<name>",
      "current_count": <int>,
      "target_count": <int>,
      "strategy": "upsample | downsample | keep",
      "rationale": "<why>"
    }
  ]
}
